lesson.menu_desc: Diplomatic Negotiation: softening language, concessions and reaching agreement -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Finding Common Ground -- Diplomatic Negotiation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 6 -- Finding Common Ground

listenings.text: Good morning, everyone, and thank you for joining this short alignment call. I want to be transparent about where the functional ingredients project stands. The lab results are promising, but our timeline is tight, and the budget is under pressure. I understand that the marketing team would prefer to launch in the spring, and that makes sense from a commercial point of view. However, from a scientific standpoint, I have some concerns about releasing data that is still preliminary. So here is what I propose. Perhaps we could split the work into two phases. In the first phase, we confirm the core results, which I am confident we can do on time. In the second phase, we expand the study. That way, we protect the science and we keep most of the original schedule. I am open to your input, and I believe that if we work together, we can find a solution that satisfies both teams. Let us discuss the details and try to reach an agreement before the end of the week.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the regulatory team. I listened to your proposal about the two-phase plan, and overall I think it is a reasonable compromise. You raise a fair point about the preliminary data, and I agree that we should not rush the publication. That said, I would like to add one condition. Before we move to the second phase, the regulatory documents must be fully reviewed, otherwise we could face a delay later. On the other hand, I am happy to be flexible on the internal deadline, so we have a little room to negotiate. Let us schedule a call tomorrow, find some middle ground, and finalize the terms together. I am confident we can meet each other halfway. Talk soon, and thank you for being so open to feedback.

extra_audio.text: First, acknowledge the other person's point of view. Next, raise your own concern in a soft and polite way. After that, propose a possible compromise. Then, ask for the other side's input. Finally, confirm the agreement and the next steps.
